content.problemStatement:
Given an array AAA of NNN integers, such that 1≤A[i]≤1001 \le A[i] \le 1001≤A[i]≤100,,

sort AAA in non-decreasing/ascending order, and display the elements of the sorted array.

Input:
The first line contains an integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, the number of elements in AAA.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers, denoting the elements of array AAA.
Output:For each test case, display the elements of the sorted array, in a new line, separated by a space.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of elements. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A[i] (1 ≤ A[i] ≤ 100).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output the sorted array in non‑decreasing order on a single line. Elements are separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: sorting [7,2,9,10,1] gives [1,2,7,9,10]. Second test case: sorting [3,7,7,3] gives [3,3,7,7].
The array is sorted into non‑decreasing order.
All elements are equal, so the output is the same sequence.

solution.approach: Because every element lies in the small range [1,100], we can sort in linear time using counting sort. For each test case we create a frequency array freq[101] initialized to zero, scan the N numbers and increment freq[value]. Then we output the numbers from 1 to 100, repeating each value freq[value] times. This yields a stable non‑decreasing order without using comparison‑based sorting.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + K) per test case, where K=100 is the size of the value range. Overall O(T·(N+100)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) = O(100) extra space for the frequency array.
